news_summary: Today's positive human rights news highlights efforts to improve housing accessibility, promote ethical AI use, combat cyberbullying against women, and empower women's civil society leadership.
news_summary_fr: Les actualités positives d'aujourd'hui en matière de droits humains mettent en avant les efforts visant à améliorer l'accessibilité au logement, à promouvoir une utilisation éthique de l'IA, à lutter contre le cyberharcèlement envers les femmes et à renforcer le leadership des femmes dans la société civile.
news_summary_es: Las noticias positivas de hoy sobre derechos humanos destacan los esfuerzos por mejorar la accesibilidad a la vivienda, promover el uso ético de la inteligencia artificial, combatir el ciberacoso contra las mujeres y empoderar el liderazgo de las mujeres en la sociedad civil.

articles.title: Press release - Tax incentives, renovation and less red tape to tackle the EU’s housing crisis
articles.summary: On Tuesday, Parliament adopted its recommendations on the housing crisis in the EU, proposing solutions for decent, sustainable and affordable housing.Special Committee on the Housing Crisis in the European Union Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
The final report of the Special Committee on the Housing Crisis, adopted by 367 votes in favour and 166 against, with 84 abstentions, emphasises that millions of Europeans face precarious living conditions due to the housing crisis, and calls for EU initiatives to help address rising prices and shortages by supporting construction and renovation.



Improving living standards



To ensure decent housing, MEPs want the Commission’s Affordable Housing Plan to earmark specific funds for renovation, to improve the energy performance of residential buildings and to combat energy poverty. All new dwellings should meet quality standards on insulation, energy efficiency, and air quality, MEPs say.

Acknowledging the recent proliferation of short-term rentals, MEPs call for the upcoming law to strike a balance between developing tourism and ensuring access to affordable housing. It should set common objectives at EU level while leaving EU-countries, regions and local authorities the flexibility to design and implement measures adapted to their specific territorial realities and housing markets, MEPs say.

MEPs also want to see an adequate share of public and social housing in EU cities to increase the affordability and supply of housing for vulnerable people.

Parliament strongly condemns squatting - the illegal occupation of houses - and demands stricter measures to protect owners. It also urges member states to strengthen the protection of tenants’ rights ensuring fair conditions and preventing disproportionate rent increases.



Investments and fiscal aid

MEPs advocate incentive-based tax measures to support low- and middle-income households, the removal of tax barriers (i.e. high registration fees) for first time buyers, and tax conditions that make long-term rentals more affordable.

The report calls for more EU investment in housing by coordinating existing funding better and reallocating unused Recovery and Resilience Plan resources to support the construction and renovation of social, public cooperative, and affordable housing.

MEPs also say any revision of state aid rules should facilitate public investment in social housing, while respecting the diversity of national markets.



Faster and easier procedures

MEPs ask the Commission for a simplification package to reduce red tape in the housing sector. They want to see simpler permitting procedures, for example digital permit-granting procedures, and a 60-day deadline for granting permits.



Strengthening the EU’s construction and renovation sector

The EU must enhance its industrial sovereignty in the construction and renovation sector, MEPs say, urging the Commission to scale up innovative and sustainable products, reinforce the single market for raw materials, and include minimum ‘Made in EU’ origin requirements for components in EU co-financed projects.

Finally, MEPs demand action to improve the working conditions of skilled workers through training and fair wages. They call for easier intra-EU labour mobility, mutual recognition of professional qualifications and, where necessary, the recruitment of skilled workers from third countries.

Quotes

Rapporteur of the housing report, Borja Giménez Larraz (EPP, ES), said: “A generation that cannot afford a home cannot build a future. Europe is short 10 million homes, rents are up by more than 30%, and young people and families are paying the price. For the first time ever, the European Parliament is setting out a roadmap: a housing simplification package, faster permits in 60 days, investment in skills, legal certainty and protection for property owners and tenants, the mobilisation of private and public investment, and stronger support for young people, families and people with disabilities. No more excuses. Member states must now deliver.”

Chair of the Housing committee, Irene Tinagli (S&D, IT), said: “Housing is a fundamental social priority, and in the past year our committee has demonstrated that the housing crisis in the EU is real, affecting people in all member states. Today, the European Parliament is taking action to address this urgent social and economic crisis by putting forward practical and innovative solutions. The housing crisis has far-reaching consequences for the quality of life of Europeans, impacting people’s health, social cohesion, and access to economic opportunities. EU action is essential to restore balance and fairness in the housing market, because everyone deserves a place to call home.”



Press conference

On Tuesday 10 March at 17.30, rapporteur Borja Giménez Larraz (EPP, ES) and Housing Committee chair Irene Tinagli (S&D, IT) will brief journalists on the outcome of the plenary vote.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the European Parliament adopting comprehensive recommendations to tackle the EU housing crisis, aiming to improve living standards, affordability, and sustainability for millions of Europeans. It includes concrete policy proposals such as tax incentives, funding for renovation, simplified permitting, and tenant protections, which have broad societal impact and substance.
articles.personality_title: European Parliament adopts new plan to ease housing crisis with tax help and faster permits
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people across the European Union (EU) struggle to find affordable and decent homes because housing prices are rising and there are not enough houses. This makes living conditions difficult for millions.

**What happened** – On Tuesday, the European Parliament voted to approve recommendations aimed at fixing the housing crisis. They want the EU to support building and renovating homes, especially to make them energy efficient and affordable. The plan includes tax breaks for buyers and renters, easier and faster permission processes for construction, and better protection for tenants and homeowners.

**Impact** – This plan is important because it offers clear, practical steps to help people get better homes. For example, it suggests setting a 60-day limit for permit approvals and using EU funds to build more public and social housing for vulnerable groups. It also aims to balance tourism with housing needs by managing short-term rentals. These measures can improve living conditions and make housing more affordable across many cities.

**What's next step** – The EU Commission will need to create and apply these new rules and funding plans. Member countries have to follow through on the recommendations to make housing easier to find and pay for. Experts will also work on improving construction quality and worker training to support these changes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The European Parliament has agreed on a detailed plan to make housing more affordable and sustainable by cutting red tape, offering tax help, and boosting renovation and construction.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen adopte un plan pour faciliter l’accès au logement avec des aides fiscales et des permis plus rapides
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreuses personnes dans l’Union européenne (UE) ont du mal à trouver des logements abordables et décents, car les prix augmentent et le nombre de logements est insuffisant. Cela rend les conditions de vie difficiles pour des millions de personnes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Mardi, le Parlement européen a voté en faveur de recommandations visant à résoudre la crise du logement. Il souhaite que l’UE soutienne la construction et la rénovation des logements, notamment pour les rendre plus économes en énergie et abordables. Le plan comprend des réductions fiscales pour les acheteurs et locataires, des procédures d’autorisation plus simples et rapides, ainsi qu’une meilleure protection des locataires et propriétaires.

**Impact** – Ce plan est important car il propose des mesures concrètes pour aider les gens à avoir de meilleurs logements. Par exemple, il suggère une limite de 60 jours pour l’approbation des permis et l’utilisation des fonds européens pour construire davantage de logements publics et sociaux destinés aux personnes vulnérables. Il vise aussi à équilibrer tourisme et besoins en logement en régulant les locations de courte durée. Ces mesures peuvent améliorer les conditions de vie et rendre le logement plus abordable dans de nombreuses villes.

**Prochaine étape** – La Commission européenne devra créer et appliquer ces nouvelles règles et plans de financement. Les pays membres devront suivre ces recommandations pour faciliter l’accès au logement et le rendre plus abordable. Des experts travailleront également à améliorer la qualité de la construction et la formation des travailleurs pour soutenir ces changements.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Parlement européen a adopté un plan détaillé pour rendre le logement plus abordable et durable en simplifiant les procédures, en offrant des aides fiscales et en renforçant la rénovation et la construction.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento Europeo aprueba un plan para aliviar la crisis de vivienda con incentivos fiscales y permisos más rápidos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas en la Unión Europea (UE) tienen dificultades para encontrar viviendas asequibles y adecuadas porque los precios suben y no hay suficientes casas. Esto hace que las condiciones de vida sean difíciles para millones.

**Qué pasó** – El martes, el Parlamento Europeo votó a favor de recomendaciones para solucionar la crisis de vivienda. Quieren que la UE apoye la construcción y renovación de viviendas, especialmente para hacerlas eficientes en energía y asequibles. El plan incluye descuentos fiscales para compradores y arrendatarios, procesos de permisos más simples y rápidos, y mejor protección para inquilinos y propietarios.

**Impacto** – Este plan es importante porque ofrece pasos claros y prácticos para ayudar a que las personas tengan mejores viviendas. Por ejemplo, propone un límite de 60 días para aprobar permisos y usar fondos de la UE para construir más viviendas públicas y sociales para grupos vulnerables. También busca equilibrar el turismo con las necesidades de vivienda regulando los alquileres de corta duración. Estas medidas pueden mejorar las condiciones de vida y hacer la vivienda más asequible en muchas ciudades.

**Próximo paso** – La Comisión de la UE deberá crear y aplicar estas nuevas reglas y planes de financiamiento. Los países miembros deben cumplir con las recomendaciones para facilitar el acceso a la vivienda y hacerla más económica. Los expertos también trabajarán en mejorar la calidad de construcción y la formación de trabajadores para apoyar estos cambios.

**Resumen en una frase** – El Parlamento Europeo acordó un plan detallado para hacer la vivienda más asequible y sostenible mediante la reducción de trámites, incentivos fiscales y el impulso a la renovación y construcción.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant European neighborhood with newly renovated, energy-efficient houses featuring visible insulation layers and solar panels, surrounded by diverse, interconnected puzzle pieces symbolizing cooperation and simplified processes, with gentle rays of light highlighting pathways leading to open doors representing accessible, affordable housing for families and communities.

articles.title: Stop the use of AI in war until laws can be agreed
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 10 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00762-yResearchers working on the most advanced AI models want rules to be drawn up to minimize the harm the technologies could cause. Their warnings need to be heard.
articles.content:
Researchers working on the most advanced AI models want rules to be drawn up to minimize the harm the technologies could cause. Their warnings need to be heard.

Researchers working at the companies behind AI tools ChatGPT, Claude and Gemini are urging the firms to stop using AI technologies for mass surveillance and in fully autonomous weapons.Credit: Jaque Silva/NurPhoto/Getty

Attacks on Iran by the United States and Israel have reminded us how close artificial-intelligence research is to the front line. Reports of AI being used to prioritize targets are not new. Every second counts in the heat of battle, and those deploying the technology think it has the potential to fire faster, or respond to incoming fire more quickly, than humans can, and with minimal need for direct communication with command centres. At last week’s Raisina Dialogue — India’s annual foreign-policy conference — Anil Chauhan, the country’s chief of defence staff, and his Philippine counterpart, Romeo Brawner, stressed that AI and automated systems are transforming warfare.

How AI is shaping the war in Iran — and what’s next for future conflicts

Many AI researchers say that even the most advanced technologies, known as frontier AI models, are not yet capable of performing reliably or within the existing laws of war. Employees at OpenAI and Google, two of the technology firms developing such models, have gone public with their concerns. Their warning must be heeded. As with other technologies that have the potential to kill indiscriminately, the use of AI in warfare should not be permitted until there are specific rules to govern that use.

At present, there are no international laws that explicitly mention AI use in war. However, international humanitarian law states clearly that weapons must not be used indiscriminately. Moreover, combatants must take precautions to verify their targets and minimize the risk of civilian casualties. These requirements should apply to AI as much as to any other military technology.

Last month, Anthropic — the firm in San Francisco, California, behind the AI model Claude — publicly fell out with the US Department of Defense (DoD). The DoD demanded the right to use Anthropic’s technology for any lawful purpose. Anthropic refused permission for its models to be used for mass surveillance or in weapons without human oversight, in line with what it says its models can “reliably and responsibly do”. The DoD cancelled Anthropic’s contract and has officially designated the firm a “supply chain risk”. This would exclude Anthropic from bidding for certain US government contracts. In response, Anthropic has sued.

AI weapons: Russia’s war in Ukraine shows why the world must enact a ban

By contrast, rival AI company OpenAI, also based in San Francisco, agreed to a version of the DoD’s terms. More than 100 of the firm’s employees, along with nearly 900 at Google, have signed an open letter calling on the two companies to refuse the government’s demands (see https://notdivided.org/). On Saturday, Caitlin Kalinowski, leader of robotics at OpenAI, resigned, saying, “AI has an important role in national security. But surveillance of Americans without judicial oversight and lethal autonomy without human authorization are lines that deserved more deliberation than they got.”

As Nature and others have reported, many researchers have ethical and moral objections to AI’s use for mass surveillance and in autonomous weapons. And some have practical concerns relating to the technology’s current capabilities. AI models can give inaccurate outputs, and how they reach their conclusions is often opaque. Incorporating AI into military technology might one day reduce civilian casualties, but the creators of some of the best AI models say that this is not yet true.

Why we should limit the autonomy of AI-enabled weapons

Many staff members joined US AI companies on the understanding that their work would not be used by the military, or would have only restricted use. But in January 2024, OpenAI updated its policies, deleting wording that said its models could not be used in “military and warfare”. Last February, Google dropped its commitment to preventing its AI being used in surveillance or weapons. And Anthropic’s chief executive, Dario Amodei, has previously suggested using AI to achieve military superiority over autocracies.

These developments clearly show the limitations of voluntary commitments, which can always be walked back. An alternative approach is to draw up a legally binding international agreement on what is acceptable. There are precedents for scientists taking the lead in such efforts. Nuclear weapons were developed with little regulation or international agreement. Accords such as the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons and the Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons came about partly as a result of initiatives led by scientists. Under the Chemical Weapons Convention, it is illegal to develop or deploy such weapons, and a similar legal agreement exists to prevent the use of biological weapons.

UN creates new scientific AI advisory panel: what will it do?

The Convention on Certain Conventional Weapons is designed to address issues relating to emerging weapons. Its member states, including the United States, have been meeting to discuss lethal autonomous weapons. This could eventually lead to the creation of an international agreement that addresses AI specifically. At present, however, the development of such an accord is hindered by a lack of support from China, Israel and the United States, and by a lack of agreement over the precise role that AI has in warfare and what counts as ‘acting autonomously’. Gathering such evidence should be the job of the United Nations Independent International Scientific Panel, which was appointed last month and is working on its first report.

According to OpenAI, the DoD is planning to convene a working group on the subject, involving military personnel, government staff members and leaders of frontier AI laboratories. Researchers at those labs must continue to press for the use of AI in warfare to be restricted, and use the power they have to advocate for global rules. Negotiating treaties takes time. Some argue that the pace of AI development means that the world cannot afford to wait for slow diplomatic solutions. That is no argument for inaction. If we never get started, it will always be too late.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on AI researchers advocating for international laws to restrict the use of AI in warfare and mass surveillance, aiming to prevent indiscriminate harm and protect civilians. This represents a significant, broad, and substantive effort to ensure ethical use of emerging technology with potential large-scale humanitarian impact. It highlights proactive steps toward global regulation and ethical responsibility in AI development, which is inspiring and hopeful for the future of human rights and warfare.
articles.personality_title: AI researchers call for global rules to limit AI use in warfare
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Artificial intelligence (AI) is being used more in military actions, such as targeting in conflicts. However, there are no international laws that clearly regulate how AI can be used in war.

**What happened** – Leading AI researchers and companies, including those behind ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini, are urging governments to stop using AI for mass surveillance and fully autonomous weapons until clear rules are made. Some companies, like Anthropic, have refused to let their AI be used without human control, even losing contracts because of it. Others, like OpenAI and Google, have faced internal protests from employees who want stricter limits.

**Impact** – This is important because AI can make mistakes and act without clear reasons, which could lead to harm to civilians. Without rules, AI weapons might be used in ways that break humanitarian laws. The call for international agreements aims to prevent uncontrolled or dangerous use of AI in war, protecting people and ensuring technology is used responsibly.

**What's next step** – The United Nations has created a scientific panel to study AI in warfare and help guide future laws. Countries are discussing possible agreements, but some major nations have not yet agreed. AI companies and researchers will continue pushing for limits, while governments plan working groups to explore the issue.

**One-sentence takeaway** – AI experts are urging the world to create clear laws that limit how AI can be used in war to keep people safer and prevent misuse.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les chercheurs en IA demandent des règles internationales pour limiter l’usage militaire de l’IA
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’intelligence artificielle (IA) est de plus en plus utilisée dans les conflits militaires, notamment pour cibler des ennemis. Pourtant, il n’existe pas de lois internationales précises sur l’utilisation de l’IA en temps de guerre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs et entreprises majeurs en IA, comme ceux derrière ChatGPT, Claude et Gemini, appellent les gouvernements à arrêter d’utiliser l’IA pour la surveillance de masse et les armes autonomes tant que des règles claires ne sont pas établies. Certaines entreprises, comme Anthropic, refusent que leur IA soit utilisée sans contrôle humain, ce qui leur a coûté des contrats. D’autres, comme OpenAI et Google, font face à des protestations internes pour renforcer les limites.

**Impact** – C’est important car l’IA peut faire des erreurs et agir sans explication claire, ce qui risque de blesser des civils. Sans règles, les armes IA pourraient violer les lois humanitaires. Cet appel à des accords internationaux vise à empêcher une utilisation dangereuse ou incontrôlée de l’IA en guerre, protégeant ainsi les personnes et assurant une utilisation responsable.

**Prochaine étape** – Les Nations Unies ont créé un groupe scientifique pour étudier l’IA dans la guerre et aider à guider les futures lois. Des discussions ont lieu entre pays, mais certains grands États ne sont pas encore d’accord. Les chercheurs et entreprises continueront de demander des limites, tandis que les gouvernements prévoient des groupes de travail sur le sujet.

**En une phrase** – Les experts en IA demandent au monde de créer des lois claires pour limiter l’usage de l’IA en guerre afin de protéger les personnes et éviter les abus.
articles.personality_title_es: Investigadores en IA piden reglas globales para limitar el uso militar de la IA
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La inteligencia artificial (IA) se usa cada vez más en conflictos militares, como para seleccionar objetivos. Sin embargo, no existen leyes internacionales claras que regulen el uso de la IA en la guerra.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores y empresas líderes en IA, como las que desarrollan ChatGPT, Claude y Gemini, piden a los gobiernos que detengan el uso de IA para vigilancia masiva y armas autónomas hasta que se establezcan reglas claras. Algunas empresas, como Anthropic, se negaron a que su IA se use sin supervisión humana, perdiendo contratos por ello. Otras, como OpenAI y Google, enfrentan protestas internas para exigir límites más estrictos.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque la IA puede cometer errores y actuar sin explicación clara, lo que podría dañar a civiles. Sin reglas, las armas con IA podrían violar leyes humanitarias. La llamada a acuerdos internacionales busca evitar un uso peligroso o descontrolado de la IA en la guerra, protegiendo a las personas y asegurando un uso responsable.

**Próximo paso** – Las Naciones Unidas crearon un panel científico para estudiar la IA en la guerra y ayudar a guiar futuras leyes. Los países están en discusiones, pero algunas grandes naciones aún no están de acuerdo. Investigadores y empresas seguirán presionando por límites, mientras los gobiernos planean grupos de trabajo sobre el tema.

**Una frase para recordar** – Expertos en IA piden al mundo crear leyes claras que limiten el uso de IA en la guerra para proteger a las personas y evitar abusos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a balanced scale made of glowing circuit patterns and delicate olive branches, resting gently over a peaceful globe, with soft, warm light illuminating the scene to symbolize the urgent need for careful rules governing AI in warfare.

articles.title: Press release - International Women’s Day: call to act against cyberbullying
articles.summary: On Tuesday, MEPs heard Jackie Fox’s testimony about the cyberbullying her daughter suffered and her subsequent work to protect children and adults against such harm.Committee on Women’s Rights and Gender Equality Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
European Parliament President Roberta Metsola said: “The courage of women who refuse to stay silent continues to shape our societies. The European Parliament will always stand with them - defending equality, combating all forms of abuse online and offline and removing the barriers that prevent women from participating fully in public life.”





Jackie Fox recounted how her daughter, a healthy young woman who was targeted by relentless physical and online abuse, ended up taking her own life. In the aftermath of losing her, Ms Fox discovered that there were no laws to protect adults or children from such bullying attacks. This motivated her to campaign for the adoption of “Coco’s law” against cyberbullying in Ireland. She implored the European Parliament to make it European law too, to protect all children and adults from similar horrific abuse.





The debate continued with one round of political speakers. They called for equality and for action to fight the reactionary forces attacking women’s rights and spreading violence. Some argued that feminism is democracy in action and that half of humanity deserves the same rights and freedoms. Other speakers stressed the importance of recognising the rights and dignity of all women, including the importance of motherhood. They also emphasised the right to safety from all forms of violence and attacks, whether in private or public spaces. Regarding digital violence, MEPs stressed that bullying and hatred have nothing to do with free speech but constitute violence that must be stopped.



Rewatch the ceremony here.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant and inspiring call to action against cyberbullying, focusing on protecting women and children from online abuse through potential European legislation. It addresses a broad societal issue with meaningful impact on human rights and safety, supported by personal testimony and political commitment.
articles.personality_title: European Parliament pushes for stronger laws to stop cyberbullying
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cyberbullying is a growing problem where people, especially women and children, face harmful attacks online. Currently, many places lack strong laws to protect victims.

**What happened** – At the European Parliament on International Women’s Day, Jackie Fox shared the heartbreaking story of her daughter, who suffered severe bullying online and offline and tragically took her own life. Jackie has been campaigning for a law called “Coco’s law” in Ireland to stop cyberbullying. She asked the European Parliament to create similar laws to protect everyone across Europe.

**Impact** – This call is important because it highlights a gap in protection against online abuse that affects many people. The European Parliament’s support shows a serious step toward making the internet safer and standing against violence, especially towards women and children.

**What's next step** – The European Parliament will discuss and work on possible new laws against cyberbullying. If passed, these laws could help protect millions of people by punishing online bullying more strictly and giving victims better support.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The European Parliament is moving toward stronger laws to protect people from harmful cyberbullying after a powerful personal story highlighted the urgent need.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen milite pour des lois plus strictes contre la cyberintimidation
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La cyberintimidation est un problème croissant où des personnes, en particulier des femmes et des enfants, subissent des attaques nuisibles en ligne. Actuellement, beaucoup d’endroits manquent de lois fortes pour protéger les victimes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Lors de la Journée internationale des femmes, Jackie Fox a partagé l’histoire bouleversante de sa fille, victime d’intimidation sévère en ligne et hors ligne, qui a tragiquement mis fin à ses jours. Jackie milite pour une loi appelée « Coco’s law » en Irlande pour stopper la cyberintimidation. Elle a demandé au Parlement européen de créer des lois similaires pour protéger tous les Européens.

**Impact** – Cet appel est important car il met en lumière un manque de protection contre les abus en ligne qui touchent beaucoup de personnes. Le soutien du Parlement européen montre une avancée sérieuse pour rendre Internet plus sûr et lutter contre la violence, surtout envers les femmes et les enfants.

**Prochaine étape** – Le Parlement européen discutera et travaillera sur de nouvelles lois possibles contre la cyberintimidation. Si elles sont adoptées, ces lois pourraient aider à protéger des millions de personnes en punissant plus sévèrement l’intimidation en ligne et en offrant un meilleur soutien aux victimes.

**En une phrase** – Le Parlement européen avance vers des lois plus fortes pour protéger les gens de la cyberintimidation après un témoignage personnel puissant qui a montré l’urgence du besoin.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento Europeo impulsa leyes más fuertes para frenar el ciberacoso
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El ciberacoso es un problema creciente donde personas, especialmente mujeres y niños, sufren ataques dañinos en línea. Actualmente, muchos lugares carecen de leyes fuertes para proteger a las víctimas.

**Qué pasó** – En el Parlamento Europeo, durante el Día Internacional de la Mujer, Jackie Fox contó la triste historia de su hija, quien sufrió acoso severo en línea y fuera de ella, y tristemente se quitó la vida. Jackie ha estado luchando por una ley llamada “Coco’s law” en Irlanda para detener el ciberacoso. Pidió al Parlamento Europeo crear leyes similares para proteger a todos en Europa.

**Impacto** – Esta petición es importante porque muestra una falta de protección contra el abuso en línea que afecta a muchas personas. El apoyo del Parlamento Europeo representa un paso serio para hacer el internet más seguro y combatir la violencia, especialmente contra mujeres y niños.

**Próximo paso** – El Parlamento Europeo discutirá y trabajará en posibles nuevas leyes contra el ciberacoso. Si se aprueban, estas leyes podrían ayudar a proteger a millones de personas, castigando más estrictamente el acoso en línea y ofreciendo mejor apoyo a las víctimas.

**En una frase** – El Parlamento Europeo avanza hacia leyes más fuertes para proteger a las personas del ciberacoso tras una historia personal que mostró la necesidad urgente.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a protective, glowing shield made of interwoven digital code strands surrounding a diverse group of abstract female silhouettes standing united, with gentle light rays breaking through dark clouds above, symbolizing courage, solidarity, and the fight against cyberbullying and violence toward women.

articles.title: ‘You are the foundation-shakers’: UN chief answers tough questions at townhall with women’s civil society groups
articles.summary: The UN chief on Tuesday applauded civil society groups for “shaking the foundations of privilege” in a male-dominated world, addressing a range of questions during a townhall meeting on the margins of the 70th session of the Commission on the Status of Women (CSW70) at UN Headquarters.
articles.content:
“You are foundation-shakers,” UN Secretary-General António Guterres told the annual townhall meeting with women’s rights defenders – which runs until 19 March – “and you are shaking out justice, dignity and equality for communities across the globe.”

Highlighting persistent power imbalances, including in politics, the economy and emerging technologies such as artificial intelligence (AI), he warned of a growing backlash against women’s rights fuelled by disinformation and culture wars.

“These are trying times,” said Mr. Guterres, who is serving his last year as the UN chief. “A bitter wind is blowing around the world. That wind is hardening attitudes and fuelling a backlash against women’s rights…that thrives on disinformation, that exploits fear and insecurity, that weaponises culture wars and that seeks to push women into silence.”

He emphasised “the last thing I want from you today is silence.”

Disquiet over merger

Representatives from around the world then took the floor, with several participants questioning the proposed merger of UN Women – the UN body leading gender equality efforts – and the UN agency for sexual and reproductive health (UNFPA).

“Why are the two main players the first target for streamlining?” asked Maria Leon Gonzalez from the Young Feminist Caucus, who represents hundreds of civil society groups, suggesting an examination of options for the merger.

To this, the UN chief said the proposal to merge the two agencies aims to create a “more powerful” one, with both mandates continuing with shared resources.

‘States must tell themselves the truth’

Law professor Joy Ngozi Ezeilo from Nigeria raised concerns about shrinking public spaces, funding shortfalls and government crackdowns and offered a solution.

“It’s time States must tell themselves the truth,” she said. “Now, it’s time to reassert that women’s rights are human rights”, a battle cry emerging from the 1995 Beijing Fourth World Conference on Women.

She said that despite gains since Beijing, “States must contribute to advancing women’s rights.”

Changing power relations

Asked about what the Secretary-General hopes to leave as his legacy, the UN chief said he underlined the importance of changing power relations to advance gender equality alongside his efforts to do so during his 10-year tenure.

UN Photo/Eskinder Debebe UN Secretary-General António Guterres answers questions at a townhall meeting with civil society groups.

Mr. Guterres also recognised the invaluable work of civil society groups, noting that the UN will convene a meeting in July to gain their input on the ongoing UN80 initiative for reform.

Defending hard-won rights for women and girls, opening new doors and building coalitions when societies are being pulled apart “is not easy”, he said.

“It comes at a price,” he stated.

“You may face threats, intimidation and harassment. When you push for change, you are pushing against the patriarchy, and the patriarchy is pushing back.”

‘You can count on me’

Pledging his support and outlining the UN’s gender equality efforts, he urged participants to “keep shaking the foundations of privilege.”

“You can count on me to be with you, now and always, and together, we will say loud and clear: no to the pushback, no to the rollback, no to going back, no surrender,” he said.

“We will never, ever give up.”

Watch the full townhall meeting on UN Web TV here:
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the UN Secretary-General's strong support for women's rights defenders globally, emphasizing the importance of advancing gender equality and human rights despite significant challenges. It reports a meaningful event with broad societal impact, focusing on ongoing efforts to combat backlash against women's rights and promote equality, with concrete context about UN initiatives and civil society engagement.
articles.personality_title: UN chief backs women’s rights defenders at key global meeting
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Every year, the United Nations holds a special meeting called the Commission on the Status of Women (CSW) to discuss women’s rights and equality. This year, during the 70th session, the UN Secretary-General met with women’s civil society groups—organizations that work to protect and promote women’s rights.

**What happened** – On March 14, UN Secretary-General António Guterres spoke at a townhall meeting with women’s rights defenders at the UN Headquarters. He praised these groups for challenging unfair systems that favor men and shared his concerns about growing attacks on women’s rights fueled by false information and cultural conflicts. He also addressed worries about merging two important UN agencies focused on gender equality and reproductive health, explaining the goal is to strengthen their work by combining resources.

**Impact** – This meeting showed strong support from the UN’s top leader for women fighting for justice worldwide. It highlighted the difficulties women face, including threats and harassment, but also the power of collective action. The Secretary-General’s promise to stand with these defenders sends a clear message that the UN values their work and will continue pushing for gender equality despite setbacks.

**What’s next step** – The UN plans to hold another meeting in July to hear more from civil society groups as part of a larger reform effort called UN80. These discussions will help guide future actions to improve support for women’s rights globally and strengthen cooperation between UN agencies and activists.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN Secretary-General reaffirmed his commitment to support women’s rights defenders worldwide, encouraging them to keep challenging inequality despite growing obstacles.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le chef de l’ONU soutient les défenseuses des droits des femmes lors d’une réunion mondiale clé
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque année, l’Organisation des Nations Unies organise une réunion spéciale appelée Commission sur la condition de la femme (CSF) pour discuter des droits des femmes et de l’égalité. Cette année, lors de la 70e session, le secrétaire général de l’ONU a rencontré des groupes de la société civile féminine – des organisations qui défendent les droits des femmes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 14 mars, António Guterres, secrétaire général de l’ONU, s’est exprimé lors d’une réunion avec des défenseuses des droits des femmes au siège de l’ONU. Il a salué ces groupes pour leur lutte contre les systèmes injustes favorisant les hommes et a exprimé ses inquiétudes face aux attaques croissantes contre les droits des femmes, alimentées par de fausses informations et des conflits culturels. Il a aussi répondu aux questions sur la possible fusion de deux agences importantes de l’ONU, expliquant que l’objectif est de renforcer leur travail en combinant leurs ressources.

**Impact** – Cette réunion a montré un fort soutien du plus haut responsable de l’ONU aux femmes qui luttent pour la justice dans le monde. Elle a mis en lumière les difficultés rencontrées par les femmes, notamment les menaces et le harcèlement, mais aussi la force de l’action collective. La promesse du secrétaire général de les accompagner envoie un message clair que l’ONU valorise leur travail et continuera à promouvoir l’égalité malgré les obstacles.

**Prochaine étape** – L’ONU prévoit une autre réunion en juillet pour écouter davantage les groupes de la société civile dans le cadre d’un effort de réforme appelé ONU80. Ces discussions aideront à guider les actions futures pour mieux soutenir les droits des femmes dans le monde et renforcer la coopération entre les agences de l’ONU et les activistes.

**En une phrase** – Le secrétaire général de l’ONU a réaffirmé son engagement à soutenir les défenseuses des droits des femmes dans le monde, les encourageant à continuer de lutter contre les inégalités malgré les obstacles croissants.
articles.personality_title_es: El jefe de la ONU apoya a defensoras de derechos de las mujeres en reunión global clave
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada año, las Naciones Unidas celebran una reunión especial llamada Comisión sobre la Condición Jurídica y Social de la Mujer (CSW) para hablar sobre los derechos de las mujeres y la igualdad. Este año, durante la 70ª sesión, el Secretario General de la ONU se reunió con grupos de la sociedad civil de mujeres, organizaciones que defienden los derechos de las mujeres.

**Qué pasó** – El 14 de marzo, António Guterres, Secretario General de la ONU, habló en una reunión con defensoras de los derechos de las mujeres en la sede de la ONU. Elogió a estos grupos por desafiar sistemas injustos que favorecen a los hombres y expresó su preocupación por el aumento de ataques contra los derechos de las mujeres, impulsados por información falsa y conflictos culturales. También respondió preguntas sobre la posible fusión de dos agencias importantes de la ONU, explicando que el objetivo es fortalecer su trabajo combinando recursos.

**Impacto** – Esta reunión mostró un fuerte apoyo del máximo líder de la ONU a las mujeres que luchan por la justicia en todo el mundo. Destacó las dificultades que enfrentan las mujeres, como amenazas y acoso, pero también el poder de la acción colectiva. La promesa del Secretario General de apoyarlas envía un mensaje claro de que la ONU valora su trabajo y seguirá impulsando la igualdad a pesar de los reveses.

**Próximo paso** – La ONU planea una nueva reunión en julio para escuchar más a los grupos de la sociedad civil como parte de un esfuerzo de reforma llamado ONU80. Estas discusiones ayudarán a guiar acciones futuras para mejorar el apoyo a los derechos de las mujeres en todo el mundo y fortalecer la cooperación entre agencias de la ONU y activistas.

**En una frase** – El Secretario General de la ONU reafirmó su compromiso de apoyar a las defensoras de los derechos de las mujeres en todo el mundo, alentándolas a seguir desafiando la desigualdad a pesar de los obstáculos crecientes.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of strong, intertwined roots breaking through cracked earth beneath a rising sun, symbolizing resilient women’s civil society groups shaking foundations of injustice and inequality, with subtle abstract representations of scales balancing and circuit-like patterns blending into the roots to evoke justice and emerging technologies—all rendered in warm, natural, earthy tones.
